transcript: Do you trust what you see on the internet today? All right. I am I'm glad that's your answer, otherwise I'll be like, what internet have you been using? Not the same one I have. So it's over, isn't it? The internet is a bit fucked, we can't really trust what we see. For example, are birds even real? Because Elon is telling me it hasn't been real since 1986, and there's 19,000 likes, so it must be true, right? Maybe yes, maybe no. We may not even have the time or the process to compute what is real on the internet because today's vanity metrics are telling us what to think. And it's easy to blame that on misinformation, you know, a lot of people say there's so much misinformation, disinformation on the internet. But we believe that is not the true problem. The true problem is the missing information. There is a huge gap between reality as we know it and what we see on the internet. And that perception then reinforces our beliefs. So this huge gap between perception and reality is what we're trying to fix. We we did, you know, we starting small with a fun fa- fun and fast-play little quiz game that has a prediction market under the hood. So a lot of us here believe that markets is what creates more valuable and more accurate information. But rest of the world do not look at charts. They are scrolling uh TikTok, they are on Candy Crush. Which is why we have this little fun game that is more of a Trojan horse, so everyone is giving their opt-in information and they know that the more honest they can be, the better their market outcomes can be. But that is allowing us to compute better context on the internet. Here is a graph about us surfacing uh like early information, hidden information about the election uh last July, by the way, before front-running any other markets out there. Um, and a lot of our users don't know what a prediction market is. They're just playing this game. Um, so far 50,000 users have used it and they've given us over 2 million contributions. Um, but here are stats that aren't on the screen that are more exciting for us. We've had thousands of over seven-day streaks, dozens of over hundred-day streaks. And over two users that have over 365-day streaks. Coming back every single fucking day, we've only been live for little over one year. And they love it because the game, earning Bonk, or whatever, but really is because they can see this final this place where they can finally find accurate and honest understanding of what people truly think. And once you can get that opt-in human context at scale, there are a lot of revenue streams we can unlock. So I won't go too much into it. Um, yeah, but you can train agents and make them more human-like, et cetera. And in the long term, we believe a lot of agents will need human reinforcement training data over and over again, and we've created this new data primitive that creates opt-in, validated, honest human input uh for all of us. Um, here's who is building it. Um, my co-founder is the thinker, I'm the doer. Uh, and together we are building the new context engine for the internet. So finally, all of us humans and agents uh can get what we deserve, which is a honest internet. Thank you.

Nov 25, 2025 10:00 AM Eastern Standard Time
# **CoPlane Announces $14M****Seed Round to Rebuild the Back Office for Essential Enterprises**
Share
*Ribbit leads round with participation from Stripe, Optum Ventures, and Terrain*
SAN FRANCISCO--([BUSINESS WIRE](https://www.businesswire.com))--CoPlane, the company building AI-native software to streamline the back office for the world’s essential enterprises, today announced its $14M seed round, led by Ribbit, with participation from Stripe, Optum Ventures, and Terrain.
ERP, or Enterprise Resource Planning, is the category of software most large companies use to manage finance, supply chain, and operations. The global ERP market is estimated at more than $80 billion, yet most systems in use today were designed decades ago. They are widely regarded as complex and costly to implement, often requiring significant consulting and manual processes to operate at scale.
CoPlane is reimagining enterprise software with an AI-native platform that collapses and replaces legacy ERP sprawl. The platform is designed for large enterprises that have long been constrained by rigid, costly back-office systems.
“Enterprise back offices haven’t meaningfully improved in decades. Across the S&P 500, G&A spend as a percentage

tags.impact_reason: A prediction market game on Solana that collects user data to train AI agents is a novel idea with potential, but its current user base and impact are still limited.

visual_idea.reasoning: I chose the Trojan Horse as the semantic anchor because it's the central metaphor Kiko Zang uses to describe CHOMP's strategy: a simple, appealing game (the horse) containing a powerful mechanism (prediction markets and data generation) to infiltrate a space (the attention economy). This visually captures the core idea of a 'deceptively simple' solution to the AI data famine. For the homepage text, I selected 'REAL?' because it directly addresses the transcript's central problem of discerning truth online ('Are birds even real?'). Displaying it as a holographic query on the horse itself makes it diegetic, framing the entire system as a machine built to answer this fundamental question.
visual_idea.description: A macro shot focusing on a glowing seam of the obsidian Trojan Horse. The camera is tight on the crack, where the sleek, dark exterior gives way to an intensely bright, intricate web of fiber optic cables and pulsating data streams in vibrant cyan and magenta. The image captures the immense power and complexity hidden just beneath the deceptively simple surface.

final_prompt.prompt_text: A macro shot, 1:1 aspect ratio, centered and symmetrical, filling the frame with intricate texture. The focus is a glowing seam on the surface of a polished obsidian Trojan Horse. The sleek, dark, highly reflective exterior of the obsidian, showcasing sharp specular highlights and deep, velvety shadows, gives way to an intensely bright, glowing crack. Within this crack, an intricate web of fiber optic cables and pulsating data streams is revealed. These internal elements emit a vibrant, neon circuitry glow in vivid cyan and magenta, resembling high-contrast vector lines of a digital blueprint. Subtle particle glow emanates from the data streams, creating an effect of immense power and complex digital information hidden just beneath the deceptively simple surface. The lighting emphasizes the dramatic contrast between the dark, reflective obsidian and the glowing, translucent internal structures. 8k, octane render, raytracing, hyper-detailed, studio lighting.

editorial.headline: CHOMP: Gamifying Prediction Markets to Feed the AI Data Famine
editorial.standfirst: Gator Labs' Kiko Zang pitches a "Trojan Horse" strategy—using a viral quiz game to generate the high-fidelity human context data that AI agents desperately need.
key_takeaways: 50,000 users have generated over 2 million data points in under a year.
Uses a "Surprisingly Popular" consensus mechanism to filter crowd bias.
Pivots from consumer game to institutional "Context Engine" for AI training (RLHF).
editorial.article_body_markdown:
The internet’s signal-to-noise ratio has collapsed. In her Superteam Demo Day pitch, **CHOMP** co-founder Kiko Zang argued that the core problem isn't misinformation, but "missing information"—the gap between reality and the vanity metrics that dominate our screens. While prediction markets are the theoretical solution to this trust deficit, their current form—complex charts and financial interfaces—alienates the mass market. The world doesn't want to trade binary options; they want to play Candy Crush.

**CHOMP** bridges this gap with a "Trojan Horse" strategy. To the user, it is a fast-paced trivia game rewarding engagement with **Bonk** and other tokens. Under the hood, however, it is a high-frequency prediction market utilizing the "surprisingly popular" algorithm. By asking users not just what they believe, but what they think *others* believe, the platform mathematically isolates honest signals from crowd sentiment. This gamification has driven sticky retention, with Zang reporting thousands of 7-day streaks and power users logging in for over 365 consecutive days.

For institutional investors, the alpha lies in the backend. Zang positions CHOMP not merely as a dApp, but as a "context engine for the internet." As AI agents proliferate, they face a critical bottleneck: a lack of nuanced, verified human context for subjective queries. CHOMP effectively acts as a decentralized **RLHF** (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback) farm. It captures and validates the messy, subjective human data that LLMs cannot scrape, creating a proprietary data primitive essential for training the next generation of more "human-like" agents.
